text: The speed at which neurotechnologies are evolving, coupled with the advent of brain data analytics enhanced with Artificial Intelligence (AI), has drawn attention to the sensitive issues of mental privacy, mental integrity and cognitive liberty. These issues are collectively known as “neurorights”, and have become increasingly discussed at an ethical, legal and policy level. Several countries have already included reference to neurorights in their Constitutions, while UNESCO and the Council of Europe are drafting guidelines and recommendations aimed at enhancing the governance of neurotechnologies. However, the discussion of neurorights currently remains divided along a multiplicity of opaque philosophical assumptions, divergent ethical stances, and contested interpretations of existing laws and regulations. This multidisciplinary research aims at filling this conceptual gap by establishing key principles in relation to mental privacy, integrity and liberty, thereby paving the way for further scholarly investigation and policy reforms at international, regional, national and subnational levels, while advancing the scholarship of neurorights through the presentation of a minimalist conceptualisation of these rights, good for future research, legislation and policy making.
explanation: Polished, high-level abstract with no concrete specifics or citations; this pattern is common in AI-generated text but can also be produced by a careful human author. The lack of distinctive human voice slightly biases toward AI likelihood.
